summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions about sexism in mathematics (regarding Fields Medal coverage and comments about Maryam Mirzakhani's appearance), career mentorship in data science, dealing with mean people in academia, and grammar debates about 'data.' The column blends personal anecdotes with sharp social commentary, particularly on how women in STEM navigate clothing choices and professional respect. O'Neil draws from her own experience leaving academia for finance to offer perspective on workplace dynamics. The overall message encourages readers to trust themselves, ignore petty meanness, and focus on doing meaningful work.
key_points: Women in STEM often dress in 'men's clothes' for practical reasons—to be taken seriously, avoid harassment, and not invite catcalls—not because of sexuality or 'having a man's brain'
Good data scientists are rare, but young analysts should trust their own integrity and learning drive rather than depending on finding a perfect mentor
Mean people exist everywhere—academia, finance, any field with scarce resources—so the goal is learning to ignore the bickering and focus on the work you love
The singular/plural 'data' debate is settled: always use singular, and anyone insisting on 'data are' sounds like a grammar nazi
money_quotes: Men's clothes are, generally speaking, great for looking totally unobjectionable, not getting harassed or hit on, and not evoking catcalls compared to short skirts and high heels, and if men could wear them they totally would. Oh wait, they already do.
Being taken seriously as a scholar comes to mind. I defend everyone's rights to trousers and a boring button-down shirt.

You have the obvious urge to do something cool and fun with your life, and because you have integrity, and because you are too smart to trick yourself into thinking what you're doing is great when it isn't.
I left academic math in part because people were so mean. They were really mean to me, and especially because I was a woman.
If you want to see hugely assholic behavior, work in finance for a few years.

As long as there are resources to be fought over, there will be fights, and the question is how to ignore all the stupid bickering and get some math done, because after all math is beautiful and awesome.
Every time I hear someone incredibly awkward like, 'Are your organization's data as clear as they can be?' I just wanna retch.
key_insight: Whether facing sexist commentary, workplace meanness, or career uncertainty, the solution is the same: trust your own integrity, ignore the noise, and focus on doing work you find meaningful.
